content:
# Disagreements — Genesis 39 (GEN_039)

## 1. Letsachek — "To Make Sport Of" vs. "To Mock" vs. "To Have His Way With" (vv. 14, 17)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "to make sport of us/me" | Pi'el of tsachaq — preserves the ambiguity (mockery + sexual sport) |
| LOGOS | "to mock us/me" | Captures the mockery/humiliation sense |
| PNEUMA | "to have his way with us/me" | Pushes toward the sexual reading |
| **SOPHIA** | **"to make sport of us/me" — EMET upheld** | The Pi'el of tsachaq covers mockery, play, and sexual sport (cf. 26:8, Exod 32:6). Potiphar's wife deploys the word's ambiguity deliberately — her accusation works on both levels. "Mock" loses the sexual dimension; "have his way with" loses the mockery dimension. "Make sport of" preserves both. |

## 2. Beit HaSohar — "Prison-House" vs. "House of Confinement" vs. "Prison" (vv. 20–23)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "prison-house" | Literal compound: beit (house) + sohar (prison/enclosure) |
| LOGOS | "house of confinement" | Interprets sohar as "confinement" — preserves Hebrew compound |
| PNEUMA | "prison" | Dynamic; modern equivalent |
| **SOPHIA** | **"prison-house" — EMET upheld** | beit haSohar is a distinctive Hebrew compound occurring only in Gen 39-40. "Prison-house" reproduces the compound literally. "House of confinement" interprets sohar's uncertain etymology. "Prison" is too modern and loses the compound structure. |

## 3. Shikbah Immi — "Lie with Me" vs. "Come to Bed with Me" (v. 7)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Lie with me" | Literal: two-word command, no softening |
| LOGOS | "Lie with me" | Same; preserves the bluntness |
| PNEUMA | "Come to bed with me" | Dynamic; adds context but softens the command |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Lie with me" — 2-1** | The Hebrew shikbah immi is deliberately stripped — two words, no social framing, no euphemism. The bluntness characterizes Potiphar's wife as commanding, not requesting. "Come to bed with me" adds furniture and reduces the force. |

## 4. Hurad — "Was Brought Down" vs. "Had Been Taken Down" (v. 1)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "was brought down" | Hophal passive of yarad — descent motif preserved |
| LOGOS | "was brought down" | Same; links to 37:36 |
| PNEUMA | "had been taken down" | Pluperfect; different verb choice ("taken" vs. "brought") |
| **SOPHIA** | **"was brought down" — 2-1** | "Brought down" is the precise rendering of the Hophal causative passive of yarad. "Taken down" loses the descent language. The identical phrase in 37:36 uses the same verb — the echo must be preserved. |

## 5. Seris — "Officer" vs. "Court Official" vs. "Courtier" (v. 1)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "court official" | Emphasizes administrative role |
| LOGOS | "officer" | Simplest rendering |
| PNEUMA | "courtier" | Emphasizes court setting |
| **SOPHIA** | **"officer" — LOGOS selected** | All three correctly avoid "eunuch." "Officer" is the most economical and combines naturally with the following title "captain of the guard." |

---

*Genesis 39: 5 disagreements logged. SOPHIA ruled with EMET on 3 items (vv. 1/hurad, 14/letsachek, 20/beit haSohar), with EMET/LOGOS jointly on 1 item (v. 7/shikbah immi), and selected LOGOS on 1 item (v. 1/seris). PNEUMA was overruled on 4 items (vv. 1, 7, 14, 20). The letsachek ruling is the most significant — preserving the deliberate ambiguity of tsachaq (Pi'el) is essential to the accusation scene's dual-register rhetoric.*
